Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty chaotic this semester. I tried to set aside time each day, but with limited resources and everything going on, I usually ended up studying in bursts whenever I could grab a few hours. I'm a visual learner, so I'd try to watch the course videos and make diagrams, but fitting in consistent study time around my other responsibilities was really hard. Most weeks I probably only managed about seven hours total, which I know wasn't enough.
Q2: My pattern varied a lot depending on what was due and what else was happening in my life. I'd have weeks where I barely touched anything, then suddenly a deadline would loom and I'd be cramming everything at once. I wanted to be more regular about it, but between anxiety about the workload and other life demands, consistency just slipped away from me. Deadlines definitely drove when I actually sat down to work.
Q3: Usually it was either a notification about an upcoming deadline or just that gnawing anxiety telling me I needed to check on something. Sometimes I'd log in just to see if there were new announcements or if I'd missed something important. I was on there pretty frequently—like 36 times over the semester—but a lot of those logins were more about checking in than deep studying. The fear of falling behind pushed me to log in more than actual motivation did.
Q4: There was a period mid-semester where logging in did become almost automatic for me. I'd wake up, make coffee, and check the LMS before doing anything else. It became a way to manage my anxiety—like if I at least looked at what was there, I felt slightly more in control. But that routine broke down when things got really overwhelming and I started avoiding it instead.
Q5: The video lectures were honestly the most useful for me because I learn best visually. I watched over 140 minutes of video content, pausing and rewinding to really understand concepts. I also found the discussion boards helpful when other students explained things in different ways than the instructor. Being able to see visual representations of concepts made a huge difference for my understanding.
Q6: I tended to skip the dense reading materials and some of the supplementary PDFs. With limited resources, I couldn't always print things out, and reading long documents on my phone was exhausting. I'd tell myself I'd get to them later, but later rarely came. Some of the optional practice exercises also got pushed aside when I was scrambling just to complete the required stuff.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could immediately see how it connected to what I'd be tested on or what I needed for assignments. If something had clear visual components or practical applications, I was more likely to engage with it fully. I also valued anything that felt manageable—if it seemed like I could actually complete it without spiraling into anxiety, I was more motivated to start.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford, honestly. Required ones carried the weight of grades and deadlines, so they got priority even when I was exhausted. Optional stuff always seemed nice in theory, but when you're already struggling to keep up with the basics and your anxiety is through the roof, 'optional' basically means 'not happening.' I wish I'd had more bandwidth for them because some looked genuinely interesting.
Q9: Absolutely. I downloaded videos and PDFs to study offline because my internet access isn't always reliable and I couldn't afford to waste data re-streaming things. I'd take handwritten notes from the videos and study from those for hours without touching the LMS. I also used free YouTube videos and other external resources to supplement what I didn't fully understand from the course materials.
Q10: There were many times I'd be sitting with my notebook, working through problems or reviewing my handwritten notes from lectures, and none of that showed up anywhere online. I'd spend hours studying from downloaded materials on the bus or at home when my internet was spotty. My actual learning time was probably double what the LMS recorded because so much happened offline.
Q11: Yes, definitely. I'd download a video once, watch it multiple times offline, and then never go back to that page on the LMS. Same with PDFs—I'd access them once to download, then study from the saved file for days. The LMS would just show one quick access, but that material might have been my main study resource for a whole week.
Q12: There was a point about two-thirds through the semester where everything just crashed down on me. My anxiety was at a peak, I was juggling six online courses, and I felt completely overwhelmed. I started avoiding the LMS because even looking at it made my chest tight. The mindfulness intervention actually helped me cope, but by then I'd already fallen behind on several assignments.
Q13: Constantly. Fatigue was a huge factor—I'd log in intending to study but just stare at the screen because I was too mentally exhausted to absorb anything. With six courses and limited resources, I was always juggling competing priorities. Some weeks I had to choose which course to neglect, and unfortunately this one sometimes drew the short straw. The cognitive overload was real.
Q14: Oh, many times. I'd have videos playing while my mind was somewhere else entirely, worrying about other assignments or just zoning out from stress. I'd scroll through discussion posts without really reading them, just going through the motions. Those sessions technically counted as 'engagement' in the system, but I wasn't actually learning anything meaningful during them.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even when I knew they were important. The anxiety made it hard to start things because I was afraid of failing or doing them wrong. I'd tell myself I'd feel more prepared tomorrow, but tomorrow I'd feel even more anxious because now there was less time. It was this terrible cycle where avoidance fed the anxiety which fed more avoidance.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to actually submit work. I'd have this last-minute surge of panic-fueled productivity right before something was due. My LMS activity probably spiked dramatically in the 24 hours before any deadline. It wasn't healthy, and my work quality suffered, but without that external pressure I struggled to overcome the inertia and anxiety.
Q17: When I got feedback, it did affect me, but not always positively. Good feedback motivated me to keep trying, but critical feedback sometimes made my anxiety worse and made me avoid that type of assignment in the future. I appreciated detailed comments, but sometimes I'd put off reading feedback because I was scared of what it might say. The delay in my response time to things was partly about that fear.
Q18: The course structure with six modules felt overwhelming from the start. Having so much content spread across the platform made it hard to know where to focus my limited study time. The structure didn't feel flexible enough for someone like me who was struggling with resources and managing multiple courses. A clearer, more linear path might have helped me stay on track better.
Q19: The visual elements like videos made engagement easier for someone like me. But the sheer volume of content and the way assignments were spread out made it harder. I also struggled when materials weren't downloadable or when things required constant internet access. Having low attendance requirements should have helped, but it actually made it easier to fall behind without noticing until it was too late.
Q20: I think that was basically me. I was logging in frequently, watching videos, posting in discussions, but my actual assignment completion was only around 55%. The high activity was partly anxiety-driven checking, partly genuine attempts to learn, but translating that into submitted work was where I broke down. Understanding material and producing graded work are two different skills, and stress made the second one nearly impossible.
Q21: That's interesting because it's kind of the opposite of my situation. I guess some students are just more efficient or they study effectively offline and only use the LMS for submitting work. They might have better resources or less anxiety interfering with their productivity. For me, high activity didn't translate to performance because so much of my engagement was fragmented by stress and competing demands.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when you're active, what you click on, how long you spend on pages. But they completely miss the quality of that engagement and what's happening offline. They can't see that I watched a downloaded video five times or that I was too anxious to focus during a logged session. They measure presence, not learning, and for students like me with complicated circumstances, that's a huge gap.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material and feeling connected to the course somehow. It's when you're curious about what you're learning, not just going through motions to check boxes. For me, real engagement happened in moments when the anxiety quieted down and I could actually absorb something new. It's mental presence, not just physical or digital presence.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I was actually trying despite everything working against me. It wouldn't show the hours of offline studying, the anxiety attacks before logging in, or how the mindfulness intervention genuinely helped me cope even if my grades didn't reflect it. It wouldn't show that I'm resilient and I kept coming back even when I was failing. My engagement was real—it just didn't always look like success in the data.
